anchorText: Wet weather was the worst; the cold, damp, clammy wet, that wrapped him up like a moist great-coat — the only kind of great-coat Toby owned,

levels.body: The passage is conspicuous because of its sound pattern. Apart from the stresses in the phrase "Wet weather was the worst" (cf. Bauer 115), the line is also marked by the alliteration of the voiced labial–velar approximant (w sound) and the voiced dental fricative (th sound). Not only do such sound patterns contribute to the overall musicality and rhythmicality in the novel, but, at this point specifically, they enhance the sensory experience (cf. Brooke 712) of reading about rain and wet weather as well. The quick repetition of the fluid "w" and "th" sounds embedded in the specific rhythm echoes the rain drops falling from the sky.
worksCited: Bauer, Matthias. "The Chimes and the Rhythm of Life." *Charles Dickens as an Agent of Change*. Ed. Joachim Frenk & Lena Steveker. New York: AMS Press, 2015, pp. 111-127.
Dickens, Charles. "The Chimes." *A Christmas Carol and Other Christmas Books*, edited by Robert Douglas-Fairhurst, OUP, 2008, pp. 85-162.
Lea, R. Brooke, et al. "Sweet Silent Thought." *Psychological Science*, vol. 19, no. 7, July 2008, pp. 709–16. Sage Journals, https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02146.x.

levels.body: Whereas in the preceding paragraph the narrator remarks upon how windy and frosty weather is akin to a holiday for Toby (cf. Bauer 114), in the paragraph under consideration here the narrator emphasizes several times the adverse effect of wet weather; he does so through highly descriptive passages and the use of alliteration. Wet and cold weather becomes a source of discomfort for Toby, and it surrounds him like a moist and misty coat. The reason for this could be the plain fact that under rainy and cold weather the perceived temperature is much lower than with snowy or windy weather which reminds Toby of the "great-coat" he could never have owned.
worksCited: Bauer, Matthias. "The Chimes and the Rhythm of Life." *Charles Dickens as an Agent of Change*. Ed. Joachim Frenk & Lena Steveker. New York: AMS Press, 2015, pp. 111-127.
Dickens, Charles. "The Chimes." *A Christmas Carol and Other Christmas Books*, edited by Robert Douglas-Fairhurst, OUP, 2008, pp. 85-162.

levels.body: The city is personified and compared to Toby with the city's throat (the streets) being choked by the mist. The reason for this is that with the industrialization and rising urbanisation the suburban periphery was reserved for the rich and upper classes who lived further away from their place of employment whereas the city centre with its many work places, such as stores, became more and more identified with the unskilled working classes (cf. Pooley 24). Thus, it is as if Toby and the city seem to merge into one another, which enhances the description of Toby's rather miserable life. The city as a public sphere and working-class people are grouped together while wealthy and upper-class people are secluded into private spheres of their own and protected from the adverse effects of nature through their isolation from the public sphere (i.e. the city). Thus, this kind of weather is not solely circumstantial in the narrative but becomes the primary source for the ruminations about Toby's miserable life, which becomes evident in the rather negative and discomforting descriptions of this kind of weather by the narrator.
worksCited: Dickens, Charles. "The Chimes." *A Christmas Carol and Other Christmas Books*, edited by Robert Douglas-Fairhurst, OUP, 2008, pp. 85-162.
Pooley, Colin G. "Updating Urban Geography: Understanding the Victorian City." *Teaching Geography*, vol. 9, no. 1, 1983, pp. 20–25. *JSTOR*, http://www.jstor.org/stable/23751064.
